questions.question: Consider the directed acyclic graph (DAG) with vertices {1,2,3,4,5,6} and edges: 1→2, 1→3, 2→4, 3→4, 4→5, 5→6. Apply DFS traversal starting from vertex 1, showing the discovery and finish times for each vertex, and verify that the graph is acyclic based on the DFS results.
keyPoints: DFS uses a stack (explicit or implicit via recursion) and explores as far as possible along each branch before backtracking
Discovery time (d) is recorded when vertex is first visited; finish time (f) is recorded when all neighbors are explored
A back edge from vertex u to v indicates a cycle if v is an ancestor of u (i.e., d[v] < d[u] < f[v])
For the given graph, topological order can also be derived by listing vertices by decreasing finish times

questions.question: Compare Breadth-First Search (BFS) and Depth-First Search (DFS) algorithms in terms of: (i) data structures used, (ii) time and space complexity, (iii) traversal order produced, and (iv) practical applications in graph analysis.
keyPoints: BFS uses a queue and explores level-by-level; DFS uses a stack (or recursion) and explores depth-wise
Both have O(V+E) time complexity; BFS requires O(V) extra space for queue, DFS requires O(V) for recursion stack
BFS produces shortest path (in unweighted graphs) and level-order traversal; DFS produces backtracking-based order
BFS is used in shortest path, level-order traversal, and network broadcast; DFS is used in cycle detection, topological sort, and maze solving

questions.question: Given the prerequisite structure for a set of university courses: Course A (no prerequisites), Course B (prerequisite: A), Course C (prerequisite: A), Course D (prerequisites: B and C), Course E (prerequisite: D), Course F (prerequisites: C and E). (a) Represent this as a directed graph and apply Kahn's algorithm for topological sorting, showing each step with the current queue and resulting order. (b) Prove that Kahn's algorithm correctly produces a topological ordering and explain what happens if the graph contains a cycle. (c) Write pseudocode for Kahn's algorithm and analyze its time and space complexity.
keyPoints: Kahn's algorithm uses indegree calculation; vertices with indegree 0 are added to queue, then removed updating neighbor indegrees
Step-by-step: Calculate indegrees → Queue initially {A} → Remove A, update B,C indegrees → Add B,C to queue → Continue until all vertices processed
If graph contains a cycle, at least one vertex will have non-zero indegree after processing, making topological sort impossible
Time complexity O(V+E) using adjacency list and queue; space complexity O(V) for queue and indegree array

questions.question: (a) Apply DFS-based topological sort on the DAG with vertices {Task1, Task2, Task3, Task4, Task5, Task6} and edges: Task1→Task3, Task1→Task4, Task2→Task3, Task2→Task5, Task3→Task5, Task3→Task6, Task4→Task6. Show step-by-step execution including push operations onto stack and the final topological order. (b) Explain how cycle detection is performed using DFS in directed graphs, distinguishing between back edges, cross edges, and forward edges with examples from the DFS tree. (c) Write recursive pseudocode for DFS with cycle detection using discovery and finish times, and prove that a cycle exists if and only if a back edge is found.
keyPoints: DFS-based topological sort: Perform complete DFS, then push vertices onto stack in order of completion (finish time), pop to get reverse topological order

Step-by-step: Visit Task1→Task3→Task5→Task6 (push), backtrack to Task3, visit already visited Task6, backtrack to Task1→Task4→Task6 (already visited), then Task2→Task3 (visited), Task2→Task5 (visited); final order: Task2, Task4, Task1, Task6, Task5, Task3

Cycle detection: During DFS, if we encounter a vertex currently in recursion stack (visited but not finished), it forms a back edge indicating cycle; cross and forward edges in DAG only go between separate branches

Proof: In DFS of directed graph, cycle exists ⇔ back edge exists: (⇒) any cycle has an edge from later to earlier visited vertex creating back edge; (⇐) back edge from u to ancestor v creates cycle u→...→v→u
